Decision Tree Project

For the Data Science and Business Analytics Post-Graduate Program at the University of Texas in Austin

November 16th, 2025

by Jacob Harper Fly


The Take Away: Decision Trees are another supervised learning model used to interpret data that represents classifications in order to determine the optimal strategy for how to respond to some particular case based on historical data. In other words, if I were given a bunch of data on how certain categories describe some objects, and if I knew whether those objects had passed or failed some test, then I could create a decision tree in order to allow someone to describe an object to me in order to have me predict whether it will pass or fail that same test.

Preface: INN Hotels Project¶

A significant number of hotel bookings are called-off due to cancellations or no-shows. The typical reasons for cancellations include change of plans, scheduling conflicts, etc. This is often made easier by the option to do so free of charge or preferably at a low cost which is beneficial to hotel guests but it is a less desirable and possibly revenue-diminishing factor for hotels to deal with. Such losses are particularly high on last-minute cancellations.

The new technologies involving online booking channels have dramatically changed customers’ booking possibilities and behavior. This adds a further dimension to the challenge of how hotels handle cancellations, which are no longer limited to traditional booking and guest characteristics.

The cancellation of bookings impact a hotel on various fronts:

  • Loss of resources (revenue) when the hotel cannot resell the room.
  • Additional costs of distribution channels by increasing commissions or paying for publicity to help sell these rooms.
  • Lowering prices last minute, so the hotel can resell a room, resulting in reducing the profit margin.
  • Human resources to make arrangements for the guests.

Objective¶

The increasing number of cancellations calls for a Machine Learning based solution that can help in predicting which booking is likely to be canceled. INN Hotels Group has a chain of hotels in Portugal, they are facing problems with the high number of booking cancellations and have reached out to your firm for data-driven solutions. You as a data scientist have to analyze the data provided to find which factors have a high influence on booking cancellations, build a predictive model that can predict which booking is going to be canceled in advance, and help in formulating profitable policies for cancellations and refunds.

Data Dictionary

  • ID: unique identifier of each booking

  • Adults #: Number of adults

  • Children #: Number of Children

  • Weekend Nights #: Number of weekend nights (Saturday or Sunday) the guest stayed or booked to stay at the hotel

  • Week Nights #: Number of week nights (Monday to Friday) the guest stayed or booked to stay at the hotel

  • The Meal Plan: Type of meal plan booked by the customer:

    • Not Selected – No meal plan selected
    • Meal Plan 1 – Breakfast
    • Meal Plan 2 – Half board (breakfast and one other meal)
    • Meal Plan 3 – Full board (breakfast, lunch, and dinner)

  • Parking Space?: Does the customer require a car parking space? (0 - No, 1- Yes)

  • The Room Type: Type of room reserved by the customer. The values are ciphered (encoded) by INN Hotels.

  • Lead Time #: Number of days between the date of booking and the arrival date

  • The Arrival Year: Year of arrival date

  • The Arrival Month: Month of arrival date

  • The Arrival Day: Date of the month

  • The Market Segment: Market segment designation.

  • Repeated Guest?: Is the customer a repeated guest? (0 - No, 1- Yes)

  • Previous Cancelations?: Number of previous bookings that were canceled by the customer prior to the current booking

  • Previous Sincerity?: Number of previous bookings not canceled by the customer prior to the current booking

  • Average Price #: Average price per day of the reservation; prices of the rooms are dynamic. (in euros)

  • Special Requests #: Total number of special requests made by the customer (e.g. high floor, view from the room, etc)

  • The Booking Status: Flag indicating if the booking was canceled or not.

In [2]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, confusion_matrix, classification_report, roc_auc_score, roc_curve, precision_recall_curve
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
from sklearn.metrics import make_scorer
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error, r2_score
import statsmodels.api as sm
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor
from statsmodels.tools.tools import add_constant
from sklearn.tree import export_text
from sklearn.tree import export_graphviz
import graphviz
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')

Mounted at /content/drive

My Toolbox¶

Below are some of the functions that I use when perfoming an analysis on decision trees:

In [3]:
def market_segmenter(data):
  market_segment_counts = data['The Market Segment'].value_counts()
  print('Market Segment Counts:\n', market_segment_counts)
  print(f"\nThe most common market segment is '{market_segment_counts.index[0]}' with {market_segment_counts.iloc[0]} bookings.")
In [4]:
def performance_checker(threshold):
  # checking which probabilities are greater than threshold
  pred_temp = lg1.predict(X_train1) > threshold
  # rounding off the above values to get classes
  pred = np.round(pred_temp)

  acc = accuracy_score(y_train, pred)  # to compute Accuracy
  recall = recall_score(y_train, pred)  # to compute Recall
  precision = precision_score(y_train, pred)  # to compute Precision
  f1 = f1_score(y_train, pred)  # to compute F1-score

  # creating a dataframe of metrics
  df_performance = pd.DataFrame(
      {"Accuracy": acc, "Recall": recall, "Precision": precision, "F1": f1,},
      index=[0],
  )
  print("Training performance:")
  return df_performance.head()
In [5]:
def heatmapper(threshold):
  y_pred = lg1.predict(X_train1) > threshold
  cm = confusion_matrix(y_train, y_pred)

  plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
  sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Blues')
  plt.ylabel("True label")
  plt.xlabel("Predicted label")
  plt.show()

Observations

  • Only 3% of customers required a parking.
  • The average customer booked 85 days in advance.
  • Most of the data was from 2018 with less that 25% from 2017.
  • Less than 3% of customers were returning guests.
  • The average price of a room is 103 euros.

Now, I will create two data sets in order to compare how the canceled booksing compared to those that weren't canceled.
In [14]:
Bad = data[data['The Booking Status'] == 'Canceled'].copy()
Good = data[data['The Booking Status'] == 'Not_Canceled'].copy()

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

Leading Questions:

  1. What are the busiest months in the hotel?
  2. Which market segment do most of the guests come from?
  3. Hotel rates are dynamic and change according to demand and customer demographics. What are the differences in room prices in different market segments?
  4. What percentage of bookings are canceled?
  5. Repeating guests are the guests who stay in the hotel often and are important to brand equity. What percentage of repeating guests cancel?
  6. Many guests have special requirements when booking a hotel room. Do these requirements affect booking cancellation?

First, let's check how the number of canceled bookings compares to those which we not canceled across all customers in the dataset.
In [15]:
booking_status_counts = data['The Booking Status'].value_counts()

print("Booking Status Counts:\n", booking_status_counts)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.barplot(x=booking_status_counts.index, y=booking_status_counts.values, palette='coolwarm')
plt.title('Counts of Booking Statuses')
plt.xlabel('Booking Status')
plt.ylabel('Number of Bookings')
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.show()

Busiest Months¶

In [16]:
def plot_months(data):
  busiest_months = data['The Arrival Month'].value_counts().sort_index()
  print('Bookings per month:\n', busiest_months)

  plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
  sns.barplot(x=busiest_months.index, y=busiest_months.values, palette='viridis')
  plt.title('Busiest Months in the Hotel')
  plt.xlabel('Month')
  plt.ylabel('Number of Bookings')
  plt.xticks(ticks=np.arange(12), labels=['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar', 'Apr', 'May', 'Jun', 'Jul', 'Aug', 'Sep', 'Oct', 'Nov', 'Dec'])
  plt.show()
plot_months(data)

Room Price by Market Segment¶

In [23]:
def price_market(data):
    avg_price_by_segment = data.groupby('The Market Segment')['Average Price #'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)
    print('\nAverage Price per Market Segment:\n', avg_price_by_segment)

    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    sns.barplot(x=avg_price_by_segment.index, y=avg_price_by_segment.values, palette='coolwarm')
    plt.title('Average Room Price by Market Segment')
    plt.xlabel('Market Segment')
    plt.ylabel('Average Price per Room (euros)')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
price_market(data)

Percentage of Canceled Bookings¶

In [26]:
cancellation_counts = data['The Booking Status'].value_counts()
total_bookings = cancellation_counts.sum()
canceled_bookings = cancellation_counts.get('Canceled', 0)

cancellation_percentage = (canceled_bookings / total_bookings) * 100

print(f"Total Bookings: {total_bookings}")
print(f"Canceled Bookings: {canceled_bookings}")
print(f"Not Canceled Bookings: {cancellation_counts.get('Not_Canceled', 0)}")
print(f"Percentage of Canceled Bookings: {cancellation_percentage:.2f}%")

Total Bookings: 36275
Canceled Bookings: 11885
Not Canceled Bookings: 24390
Percentage of Canceled Bookings: 32.76%
In [27]:
repeated_guests_data = data[data['Repeated Guest?'] == 1]

repeated_guest_cancellation_counts = repeated_guests_data['The Booking Status'].value_counts()

total_repeated_guest_bookings = repeated_guest_cancellation_counts.sum()
canceled_repeated_guest_bookings = repeated_guest_cancellation_counts.get('Canceled', 0)

if total_repeated_guest_bookings > 0:
    percentage_canceled_repeated_guests = (canceled_repeated_guest_bookings / total_repeated_guest_bookings) * 100
    print(f"Total bookings by repeated guests: {total_repeated_guest_bookings}")
    print(f"Canceled bookings by repeated guests: {canceled_repeated_guest_bookings}")
    print(f"Percentage of canceled bookings by repeated guests: {percentage_canceled_repeated_guests:.2f}%")
else:
    print("No bookings found for repeated guests.")

Total bookings by repeated guests: 930
Canceled bookings by repeated guests: 16
Percentage of canceled bookings by repeated guests: 1.72%

Special Requests¶

In [28]:
# Grouping by 'Special Requests #' and 'The Booking Status' to count occurrences
special_requests_cancellation = data.groupby(['Special Requests #', 'The Booking Status']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)

# Cancellation percentage for each number of special requests
special_requests_cancellation['Total'] = special_requests_cancellation['Canceled'] + special_requests_cancellation['Not_Canceled']
special_requests_cancellation['Cancellation_Percentage'] = (special_requests_cancellation['Canceled'] / special_requests_cancellation['Total']) * 100

print("Cancellation rates based on number of special requests:\n")
display(special_requests_cancellation)

# The cancellation percentage
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(x=special_requests_cancellation.index, y=special_requests_cancellation['Cancellation_Percentage'], palette='coolwarm')
plt.title('Cancellation Percentage by Number of Special Requests')
plt.xlabel('Number of Special Requests')
plt.ylabel('Cancellation Percentage (%)')
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.show()

# The count of Canceled vs Not_Canceled for each special request category
special_requests_cancellation[['Canceled', 'Not_Canceled']].plot(kind='bar', stacked=True, figsize=(10, 6), colormap='viridis')
plt.title('Booking Status by Number of Special Requests')
plt.xlabel('Number of Special Requests')
plt.ylabel('Number of Bookings')
plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.legend(title='Booking Status')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Cancellation rates based on number of special requests:
The Booking Status Canceled Not_Canceled Total Cancellation_Percentage
Special Requests #
0 8545 11232 19777 43.206755
1 2703 8670 11373 23.766816
2 637 3727 4364 14.596700
3 0 675 675 0.000000
4 0 78 78 0.000000
5 0 8 8 0.000000 
/tmp/ipykernel_3795/579808034.py:13: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(x=special_requests_cancellation.index, y=special_requests_cancellation['Cancellation_Percentage'], palette='coolwarm')

It doesn't look like there is much of a correlation between the number of special requests and the likelihood of cancellation.

Bivariate Analysis - At A Glance¶

In [29]:
cols_list = data.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns.tolist()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
sns.heatmap(
    data[cols_list].corr(), annot=True, vmin=-1, vmax=1, fmt=".2f", cmap="Spectral"
)
plt.show()

Observations

  • Guests who recieve lower prices are more likely to be returning guests.
  • Guests are more likely to cancel reservations made with a greater lead time, a smaller number of special requests and on weekend nights.

Data Preprocessing¶

  • Missing value treatment (if needed)
  • Feature engineering (if needed)
  • Outlier detection and treatment (if needed)
  • Preparing data for modeling
  • Any other preprocessing steps (if needed)

But first, I will add a column of dates.
In [30]:
TimedData = data.copy()
TimedData['Arrival Period'] = pd.to_datetime(TimedData[['The Arrival Year', 'The Arrival Month']].assign(day=15).rename(columns={'The Arrival Year': 'year', 'The Arrival Month': 'month'}))
display(TimedData.head())
Adults # Children # Weekend Nights # Week Nights # The Meal Plan Parking Space? The Room Type Lead Time # The Arrival Year The Arrival Month The Arrival Day The Market Segment Repeated Guest? Previous Cancellations # Previous Sincerity # Average Price # Special Requests # The Booking Status Arrival Period
0 2 0 1 2 Meal Plan 1 0 Room_Type 1 224 2017 10 2 Offline 0 0 0 65.00 0 Not_Canceled 2017-10-15
1 2 0 2 3 Not Selected 0 Room_Type 1 5 2018 11 6 Online 0 0 0 106.68 1 Not_Canceled 2018-11-15
2 1 0 2 1 Meal Plan 1 0 Room_Type 1 1 2018 2 28 Online 0 0 0 60.00 0 Canceled 2018-02-15
3 2 0 0 2 Meal Plan 1 0 Room_Type 1 211 2018 5 20 Online 0 0 0 100.00 0 Canceled 2018-05-15
4 2 0 1 1 Not Selected 0 Room_Type 1 48 2018 4 11 Online 0 0 0 94.50 0 Canceled 2018-04-15

Now, to check for outliers.

In [ ]:
numerical_cols = data.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns.tolist()

# Exclude ID if it's considered purely an identifier and not a numerical feature for distribution analysis
if 'ID' in numerical_cols:
   numerical_cols.remove('ID')

# Determine grid size
num_plots = len(numerical_cols)
if num_plots == 0:
    print("No numerical columns found to plot.")
else:
    # Adjust figsize and grid layout based on the number of plots
    n_cols = 3 # Number of columns for subplots
    n_rows = (num_plots + n_cols - 1) // n_cols # Calculate number of rows needed

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(n_rows, n_cols, figsize=(n_cols * 5, n_rows * 4))
    axes = axes.flatten() # Flatten the 2D array of axes for easy iteration

    for i, col in enumerate(numerical_cols):
        sns.boxplot(y=data[col], ax=axes[i])
        axes[i].set_title(col)
        axes[i].set_ylabel('') # Remove y-label to avoid clutter

    # Hide any unused subplots
    for j in range(i + 1, len(axes)):
        fig.delaxes(axes[j])

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.suptitle('Box Plots of Numerical Columns', y=1.02, fontsize=16)
    plt.show

While there are a great number of outliers; however, what the data represents is true; therefore, I will not treat them.


Now, I will split the data into train and test data so that I can both create a model and evaluate that model.
In [ ]:
X = data.drop(["The Booking Status"], axis=1)
Y = data["The Booking Status"].map({'Canceled': 1, 'Not_Canceled': 0})

# adding constant
X = sm.add_constant(X)

X = pd.get_dummies(X, drop_first=True)

# Splitting data in train and test sets
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, Y, test_size=0.30, random_state=1
)

X_train,X_test = X_train.astype('float'),X_test.astype('float')

print("Shape of Training set : ", X_train.shape)
print("Shape of test set : ", X_test.shape)
print("Percentage of classes in training set:")
print(y_train.value_counts(normalize=True))
print("Percentage of classes in test set:")
print(y_test.value_counts(normalize=True))

The code ran as expected, and now, I can build the logistic regression model.

In [ ]:
logit = sm.Logit(y_train, X_train.astype(float))
lg = logit.fit(disp=False)

print(lg.summary())

Observations

  • People are less likely to cancel if they require a parking space, have booked in the past, have a great number of special requests, and/or are booked offline or by a corporation.

Multicollinearity¶

Given that these variables might contain multicollinearity, or a variable that is too highly correlated to others, the p-values shown may not be valid. As a result, I will look for high Variation Inflation Factors (VIFs) in order to test for which variables may need to be dropped from the dataset.

In [ ]:
vif = pd.DataFrame()
vif["feature"] = X_train.columns
vif["VIF"] = [
    variance_inflation_factor(X_train.values, i)
    for i in range(len(X_train.columns))
    ]

Ignoring the VIF of dummy variables, there is no significant multicollinearity in the data.

Dropping The Insignificant¶

The following loop will go about creating a model, dropping the feature with the highest p-value, then repeating the process until there are no features with a p-value greater than 0.05

In [ ]:
# initial list of columns
cols = X_train.columns.tolist()

# setting an initial max p-value
max_p_value = 1

while len(cols) > 0:
    # defining the train set
    x_train_aux = X_train[cols]

    # fitting the model
    model = sm.Logit(y_train, x_train_aux).fit(disp=False)

    # getting the p-values and the maximum p-value
    p_values = model.pvalues
    max_p_value = max(p_values)

    # name of the variable with maximum p-value
    feature_with_p_max = p_values.idxmax()

    if max_p_value > 0.05:
        cols.remove(feature_with_p_max)
    else:
        break

selected_features = cols
print(selected_features)
In [ ]:
X_train1 = X_train[selected_features]
X_test1 = X_test[selected_features]
logit1 = sm.Logit(y_train, X_train1.astype(float))
lg1 = logit1.fit(disp=False)
print(lg1.summary())

Model Interpretation
Most Likely Causes of Cancelation


Most Likely Causes of Fulfilled Bookings

Model Evaluation¶

Assuming that it is just as important to avoid underbooking as well as overbooking, the optimal strategy is to find the maximum F1 score.

In [ ]:
performance_checker(0.5)
In [ ]:
heatmapper(0.5)

ROC-AUC¶

The ROC-AUC graph stands for the Receiver Operating Characteristic's Area Under the Curve. In short, it shows how sensative the data becomes (how likely true positives are) when the likelihood of a false positive becomes greater.

In [ ]:
logit_roc_auc_train = roc_auc_score(y_train, lg1.predict(X_train1))
fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(y_train, lg1.predict(X_train1))
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label="Logistic Regression (area = %0.2f)" % logit_roc_auc_train)
plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], "r--")
plt.xlim([0.0, 1.0])
plt.ylim([0.0, 1.01])
plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.title("Receiver operating characteristic")
plt.legend(loc="lower right")
plt.show()

An ROC-AUC score of 0.86 is pretty good, but let's see if the curve can be used to improve the model.

In [ ]:
# The optimal cut off would be where tpr is high and fpr is low
fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(y_train, lg1.predict(X_train1))

optimal_idx = np.argmax(tpr - fpr)
optimal_threshold_auc_roc = thresholds[optimal_idx]
print(optimal_threshold_auc_roc)
In [ ]:
performance_checker(optimal_threshold_auc_roc)
In [ ]:
heatmapper(optimal_threshold_auc_roc)

Perhaps the Precision-Recall curve will produce a better threshold.

In [ ]:
y_scores = lg1.predict(X_train1)
prec, rec, tre = precision_recall_curve(y_train, y_scores,)

plt.plot(tre, prec[:-1], "b--", label="Precision")
plt.plot(tre, rec[:-1], "g--", label="Recall")
plt.xlabel("Threshold")
plt.ylabel("Score")
plt.legend(loc="upper left")
plt.ylim([0, 1])

# Calculate the intersection point
# Find the threshold where precision and recall are closest
optimal_idx_pr = np.argmin(np.abs(prec[:-1] - rec[:-1]))
optimal_threshold_pr = tre[optimal_idx_pr]

plt.axvline(optimal_threshold_pr, color='r', linestyle=':', label=f'Intersection: {optimal_threshold_pr:.2f}')
plt.legend(loc="lower left")

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))
plt.show()

print(f"The approximate intersection point (threshold) for Precision and Recall is: {optimal_threshold_pr:.4f}")

And let's see how using the AUC-ROC score of 0.42 works as a threshold.

In [ ]:
performance_checker(0.42)
In [ ]:
heatmapper(0.42)

The F1 score is not greater than 0.7, and the heatmap isn't looking much better than before, so I will use the decision tree produced by setting to the threashold determined by the AUC-ROC.

Building a Decision Tree model¶

In [ ]:
# Initialize the Decision Tree Classifier
dt_model = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)

# Fit the model to the training data
dt_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
In [ ]:
# Make predictions on the test set
y_pred_dt = dt_model.predict(X_test)

# Evaluate model performance
print("\n--- Decision Tree Model Evaluation ---")
print(f"Accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_dt):.4f}")
print(f"Precision (Canceled): {precision_score(y_test, y_pred_dt, pos_label=1):.4f}")
print(f"Recall (Canceled): {recall_score(y_test, y_pred_dt, pos_label=1):.4f}")
print(f"F1-Score (Canceled): {f1_score(y_test, y_pred_dt, pos_label=1):.4f}")

print("\nConfusion Matrix:")
cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_dt)
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Blues', xticklabels=['Not Canceled', 'Canceled'], yticklabels=['Not Canceled', 'Canceled'])
plt.xlabel('Predicted')
plt.ylabel('Actual')
plt.title('Confusion Matrix - Decision Tree')
plt.show()

The decision tree seems to be overfitting the data. To begin considering how to prune the tree, I'll evaluate the importance of its features:

In [ ]:
feature_names = dt_model.feature_names_in_.tolist()
importances = dt_model.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8))
plt.title("Feature Importances")
plt.barh(range(len(indices)), importances[indices], color="violet", align="center")
plt.yticks(range(len(indices)), [feature_names[i] for i in indices])
plt.xlabel("Relative Importance")
plt.show()
In [ ]:
# 1. Define the hyperparameter grid
param_grid = {
    'criterion': ['gini', 'entropy'],
    'max_depth': [5, 10, 15, None], # None means unlimited depth
    'min_samples_split': [2, 5, 10],
    'min_samples_leaf': [1, 5, 10]
}

# 2. Create a custom scorer for precision, focusing on the 'Canceled' class
# Assuming 1 represents 'Canceled' based on previous processing (data['The Booking Status'] = ... if x == 'Canceled' else 0)
precision_scorer = make_scorer(precision_score, pos_label=1, average='binary')

# 3. Initialize GridSearchCV
# dt_model was initialized in a previous cell as DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)
grid_search = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=dt_model,
    param_grid=param_grid,
    scoring=precision_scorer, # Use the custom precision scorer
    cv=5,
    n_jobs=-1, # Use all available processors
    verbose=2
)

# 4. Fit GridSearchCV to the training data
grid_search.fit(X_train, y_train)

# 5. Print the best parameters and best score
print("Best Parameters found by GridSearchCV:", grid_search.best_params_)
print("Best Score (Precision for Canceled class) found by GridSearchCV:", grid_search.best_score_)

Train Tuned Decision Tree Model¶

A new Decision Tree classifier using the best hyperparameters found during the tuning process.

In [ ]:
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier

# Access the best parameters
best_params = grid_search.best_params_

# Initialize a new DecisionTreeClassifier with the best parameters
tuned_dt_model = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1, **best_params)

# Fit the tuned model to the training data
tuned_dt_model.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("Tuned Decision Tree model trained successfully with best parameters:")
print(best_params)

Now that the tuned Decision Tree model is trained, the next step is to evaluate its performance on the test set to determine how well it generalizes and to compare it with the untuned model. This involves calculating common classification metrics and visualizing the confusion matrix.

In [ ]:
# Make predictions on the test set using the tuned model
y_pred_tuned_dt = tuned_dt_model.predict(X_test)

# Evaluate model performance
print("\n--- Tuned Decision Tree Model Evaluation ---")
print(f"Accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned_dt):.4f}")
print(f"Precision (Canceled): {precision_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned_dt, pos_label=1):.4f}")
print(f"Recall (Canceled): {recall_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned_dt, pos_label=1):.4f}")
print(f"F1-Score (Canceled): {f1_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned_dt, pos_label=1):.4f}")

print("\nConfusion Matrix:")
cm_tuned = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_tuned_dt)
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.heatmap(cm_tuned, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Blues', xticklabels=['Not Canceled', 'Canceled'], yticklabels=['Not Canceled', 'Canceled'])
plt.xlabel('Predicted')
plt.ylabel('Actual')
plt.title('Confusion Matrix - Tuned Decision Tree')
plt.show()

print("\nClassification Report:")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_tuned_dt))

To compare the performance of the initial and tuned Decision Tree models, I will extract and store the key evaluation metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score for the 'Canceled' class) for both models from the previously generated predictions and then display them in a comparison table.

In [ ]:
# Metrics for Initial Decision Tree Model (from y_pred_dt)
initial_dt_metrics = {
    'Accuracy': accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_dt),
    'Precision (Canceled)': precision_score(y_test, y_pred_dt, pos_label=1),
    'Recall (Canceled)': recall_score(y_test, y_pred_dt, pos_label=1),
    'F1-Score (Canceled)': f1_score(y_test, y_pred_dt, pos_label=1)
}

# Metrics for Tuned Decision Tree Model (from y_pred_tuned_dt)
tuned_dt_metrics = {
    'Accuracy': accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned_dt),
    'Precision (Canceled)': precision_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned_dt, pos_label=1),
    'Recall (Canceled)': recall_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned_dt, pos_label=1),
    'F1-Score (Canceled)': f1_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned_dt, pos_label=1)
}

# Create a DataFrame for comparison
comparison_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Initial DT': initial_dt_metrics,
    'Tuned DT': tuned_dt_metrics
})

print("\n--- Model Performance Comparison ---")
display(comparison_df.round(4))

The Second Model seems to be doing much better - especially with regards to precision. I will use the second model because it will decrease the chances that someone is expecting a room and arrives to find out that it has been resold.

The Best Decision Tree¶

In [ ]:
# Get feature names from X_train
feature_names = X_train.columns.tolist()

# Define class names
class_names = ['Not_Canceled', 'Canceled']

# Generate DOT data
dot_data = export_graphviz(
    tuned_dt_model,
    out_file=None,
    feature_names=feature_names,
    class_names=class_names,
    filled=True,
    rounded=True,
    special_characters=True,
    impurity=False
)

# Create a graphviz.Source object and render it
graph = graphviz.Source(dot_data)
graph

The decision tree above is interesting but hard to read. Below, I have included a written version of the tree for whatever programming applications the hotel may want to develop using the model.

In [ ]:
# Get feature names from X_train (ensure 'const' is removed if it's not a real feature)
# We'll use the feature_names that were already prepared for graphviz
feature_names = X_train.columns.tolist()

# Define class names
class_names = ['Not_Canceled', 'Canceled']

# Generate the textual representation of the decision tree
tree_rules = export_text(tuned_dt_model, feature_names=feature_names, class_names=class_names)

print("--- Textual Decision Tree Rules ---")
print(tree_rules)
In [ ]:
# Access feature_importances_ attribute
feature_importances = tuned_dt_model.feature_importances_

# Create a Pandas Series using these importances, mapping them to the feature names from X_train.columns
# Ensure feature_names matches the order of features used in training
feature_names = X_train.columns.tolist()
importance_series = pd.Series(feature_importances, index=feature_names)

# Listing important features
sorted_importance = importance_series.sort_values(ascending=False)
print("Top 10 Feature Importances:")
print(sorted_importance.head(10))

# Create a bar plot to visualize the top features
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
sns.barplot(x=sorted_importance.head(10).values, y=sorted_importance.head(10).index, palette='viridis')
plt.title('Top 10 Feature Importances from Tuned Decision Tree Model')
plt.xlabel('Feature Importance')
plt.ylabel('Feature Name')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Actionable Insights and Recommendations¶

  • What profitable policies for cancellations and refunds can the hotel adopt?
  • What other recommedations would you suggest to the hotel?

My recommendation is that the price of rooms should be tied to the lead time given for the reservation - and the equation that determines price should also be considerate of month, charging more in the months between November and January.

I also recommend that the hotel run each reservation through the decision tree model provided above in order to estimate the supply of rooms when determining the price that they should be sold for given some equation for determining demand.

In [ ]:
!jupyter nbconvert --to html '/content/drive/MyDrive/Data Analytics/Decision Tree/Decision Tree Project.ipynb' --output-dir '/content/drive/MyDrive/Data Analytics/Portfolio'
